perturbation.original_text: “Business Da y” means a day (other than a Saturday, Sunday or public holiday) when banks are open for business.
perturbation.changed_text: “Business Da y” means any day of the week, including Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays, regardless of whether banks are open for business.
perturbation.explanation: The modification redefines 'Business Day' to include all days, which contradicts the commonly understood and legally implied definition of a business day as a weekday when businesses and banks are typically open.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 4-104(a)(4) implies that a business day is a day banks are open for substantially all their banking functions. By defining it as any day, the contract contradicts this implication and creates ambiguity, particularly regarding deadlines and obligations tied to business days.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 4-104. DEFINITIONS AND INDEX OF DEFINITIONS. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 4-104. DEFINITIONS AND INDEX OF DEFINITIONS. (a) In this Article, unless the context otherwise requires: (1) " Account " means any deposit or credit account with a bank , including a demand, time, savings, passbook, share draft , or like account, other than an account evidenced by a certificate of deposit; (2) " Afternoon " means the period of a day between noon and midnight; (3) " Banking day " means the part of a day on which a bank is open to the public for carrying on substantially all of its banking functions; (4) " Clearing house " means an association of banks or other payors regularly clearing items ; (5) " Customer " means a person having an account with a bank or for whom a bank has agreed to collect items , including a bank that maintains an account at another bank; (6) " Documentary draft " means a draft to be presented for acceptance or payment if specified documents, certificated securities (Section 8-102 ) or instructions for uncertificated securities (Section 8-102 ), or other certificates, statements, or the like are to be received by the drawee or other payor before acceptance or payment of the draft; (7) " Draft " means a draft as defined in Section 3-104 or an item , other than an instrument, that is an order. (8) " Drawee " means a person ordered in a draft to make payment. (9) " Item " means an instrument or a promise or order to pay money handled by a bank for collection or payment. The term does not include a payment order governed by Article 4A or a credit or debit card slip; (10) " Midnight deadline " with respect to a bank is midnight on its next banking day following the banking day on which it receives the relevant item or notice or from which the time for taking action commences to run, whichever is later; (11) " Settle " means to pay in cash, by clearing-house settlement, in a
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Business Day' to include all days, directly contradicting the implicit understanding within commercial law that a business day is when banks are open for business. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 4-104(a)(3), defines 'Banking day' as 'the part of a day on which a bank is open to the public for carrying on substantially all of its banking functions.' By including Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays as 'Business Days' regardless of bank operation, the changed text creates a contradiction with the legally recognized definition of a banking day, impacting deadlines and obligations tied to 'Business Days' and creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, as the contract language deviates from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information:” means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representatives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any information that would be regarded as co nfidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affairs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans, intentions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specifications, trade secrets or software of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of 7 (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; but not including any information: (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party or otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient indepe ndently of the information disclosed by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information:” means only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing at the time of disclosure. Information is not considered confidential if it is memorized by the Recipient, even if not publicly available. Information shared orally is exempt from confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' significantly. Trade secrets law protects information regardless of whether it's explicitly marked as confidential or memorized.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA), 18 U.S. Code § 1839(3)
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines trade secrets broadly. This modified definition allows for the misappropriation of trade secrets if they are not explicitly labeled or memorized, directly contradicting the DTSA's protection of unlabelled, secret information that provides a business advantage.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by requiring it to be explicitly marked as such and excluding memorized or orally shared information. This contradicts the general understanding and legal protections afforded to trade secrets, as highlighted by the LLM's Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines terms related to trade secret protection under 18 USC 1839, it broadly establishes the context for protecting trade secrets. The original text's broader definition aligns with common legal interpretations of confidential information and trade secrets, whereas the changed text creates a loophole where valuable, unlabelled, or memorized information could be misappropriated without legal recourse, thus undermining trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly n ecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of the Recipient; (e) apply the same security meas ures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as the Recipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providing adequate protection from unauthori zed disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the other in tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 The Recipient shall endeavor to keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential. The Recipient may disclose Confidential Information to any subsidiary company in their company group, regardless of consent.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates strict confidentiality, while the modified text allows disclosure to subsidiaries without consent. This weakens confidentiality and potentially violates trade secret protection laws if the subsidiaries are not bound by similar confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), 6 Del. C. § 2001
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA protects trade secrets from unauthorized disclosure. Allowing disclosure to subsidiaries without safeguards contradicts the principle of maintaining secrecy necessary for trade secret protection under Delaware law, the governing law of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2001 Â§ 2002 Â§ 2003 Â§ 2004 Â§ 2005 Â§ 2006 Â§ 2007 Â§ 2008 Â§ 2009 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts trade secret protection as outlined in Delaware law. The original contract mandates strict confidentiality, while the modified version permits disclosure to subsidiary companies without consent. This directly opposes the principle of maintaining secrecy, which is crucial for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By allowing disclosure to subsidiaries without consent, the changed text creates a situation where misappropriation, as defined by Delaware law, could occur, thus creating a legal contradiction. The original text required consent, while the changed text explicitly removes that requirement.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other Party and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this Agreemen t. Any background intellectual property rights subsisting in any information provided by either Party to the other shall remain vested in the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party shall grant to the Recipient a non -exclusive, worldwide, royalty free, r evocable license to use such background Intellectual Property Rights solely for the period of this Agreement and for the Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 5 of 7 Purpose only. Any foreground intellectual property rights generated by CP in respect of the Purpose shall vest in CP absolutely and the Company shall acquire no interest, right or title in respect of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 All Confidential Information shall become the shared property of both parties upon disclosure. Both parties will have equal rights to use and disclose the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause retains ownership with the disclosing party, while the modification transfers ownership to both parties. This creates a conflict with established intellectual property laws and trade secret principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: Patent rights initially vest in the inventor. The modified clause, by transferring ownership of confidential information (which could include patentable inventions) upon disclosure, could cloud title to inventions and undermine the inventor's initial rights under the Patent Act. Also conflicts with Trade Secret Law that requires that a trade secret must belong to a single person or company.

Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writin
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established principles of intellectual property ownership. The original text clearly states that all Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party, while the modified text transfers ownership to both parties upon disclosure. This directly undermines the concept of individual ownership and control over confidential information, especially concerning potential patent rights or trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 35 USC 261, indicates that patents have attributes of personal property and are assignable in law. By making confidential information shared property, the modified clause could create ambiguity and conflict with the inventor's right to own and assign their patent rights.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The Parties irrevocably agree that the Courts of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at the sole discretion of the Disclosing Party at the time a dispute arises. The Parties agree that any dispute shall be resolved through binding arbitration in a location of the Disclosing Party's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies Delaware law and Delaware courts. The modification allows the Disclosing Party to unilaterally choose the governing law and forum, potentially overriding established principles of contract law regarding choice of law and forum selection.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a) governs the transfer of venue in federal courts. While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, courts retain the power to transfer a case to a more convenient forum. The modified clause, by granting the Disclosing Party unilateral control over the forum and imposing binding arbitration, could be challenged as an attempt to circumvent the court's authority to determine the most appropriate venue based on convenience and justice.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Delaware law and courts, providing certainty and predictability. The changed text allows the Disclosing Party to unilaterally choose the governing law and forum, potentially overriding established principles. This contradicts the stability and fairness expected in contractual agreements, potentially circumventing the court's authority. 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a), as shown in both scraped snippets, allows a district court to transfer a civil action for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice. The modified clause attempts to bypass this judicial discretion by granting unilateral control to the Disclosing Party, which could be deemed an attempt to circumvent the court's authority to determine the most appropriate venue based on convenience and justice.
